front: What is ABG stepwise interpretation?
back: pH → PaCO₂ → HCO₃⁻ → Primary → Compensation → AG → ΔΔ

front: What is Winter's formula?
What is Anion gap?
What is Albumin-corrected AG?
What is MUDPILES?
back: Methanol, Uraemia, DKA, Paraldehyde, Iron/Isoniazid, Lactic, EG, Salicylate

front: What is HyperK emergency ladder?
back: Ca gluconate 10 mL → Insulin 10U + 25g dextrose → Salbutamol → Dialysis

front: What is HyperK ECG progression?
back: Peaked T → Wide QRS → Sine wave → VF

front: What is Hyponatraemia correction limits?
What is HyperNa correction limit?
What is SIADH treatment?
back: Fluid restriction 800–1000 mL/day (chronic); 3% saline if severe symptoms

front: What is HypoCa emergency?
back: Ca gluconate 10 mL IV over 10 min (QT prolongation)

front: What is HyperCa emergency?
back: IV 0.9% saline → Loop diuretic → Bisphosphonate (Zoledronic 4 mg)

front: What is HypoMg?
What is Osmotic demyelination?
back: Caused by rapid correction of chronic hyponatraemia (>10–12 mmol/L/24h)

front: What are the DKA criteria?
back: Glucose >11, pH <7.30, HCO₃ <15, ketones ≥3 or ketonuria +++

front: What are the HHS criteria?
back: Glucose >33, pH >7.30, HCO₃ >18, osm >320, minimal ketones

front: What is Initial fluid in DKA?
What is Insulin in DKA?
What is K⁺ in DKA?
back: Add 20–40 mmol/h if K⁺ 3.5–5.5; hold insulin if K⁺ <3.3

front: When to add dextrose?
What is HHS vs DKA fluid?
What is Cerebral oedema risk?
back: Age <5, new T1DM, severe acidosis, high BUN, bicarbonate use

front: What is Cerebral oedema Rx?
What is Bicarbonate in DKA?
What is Corrected Na⁺?
What is DKA resolution?
TCA cardiotoxicity antidote?
back: Sodium bicarbonate 1-2 mEq/kg bolus → target pH 7.50-7.55, QRS < 100ms. Alkalemia ↑ protein binding, Na⁺ overcomes channel block.

front: BB/CCB cardiogenic shock?
back: BB: Glucagon 5-10mg IV + infusion + HIET. CCB: Calcium + HIET. Both: vasopressors (NE) for refractory shock.

front: Salicylate enhanced elimination?
back: Urinary alkalinization (urine pH 7.5-8.0, replace K⁺). HD if pH700(acute)/>400(chronic).

front: Lithium enhanced elimination?
back: Hemodialysis. Acute >4mmol/L, Chronic >2.5 + neuro/renal/elderly. Lithium rebounds post-HD (redistributes).

front: Serotonin syndrome antidote?
back: Cyproheptadine 12mg PO/NG then 2mg q2h PRN. Supportive: benzos, cooling, paralysis if severe. Stop serotonergic drugs.

front: OP vs carbamate difference?
back: OP: phosphorylates AChE → irreversible (aging in hours). Carbamate: carbamylates → reversible, spontaneous reactivation. OP needs pralidoxime early.

front: Atropine dosing OP?
back: NO MAX DOSE. Start 1-2mg IV, repeat q5-10min. Titrate to: dry secretions, HR > 80, no bronchospasm. May need hundreds of mg.

front: Intermediate syndrome (IMS)?
back: Day 1-4. Proximal weakness, neck flexors, cranial nerves, respiratory failure. As acute crisis resolves. Ventilation needed. Not prevented by pralidoxime.

front: Carbamate + pralidoxime?
back: Usually NOT needed (spontaneous reactivation). Atropine for symptoms. May worsen some carbamates.

front: Cholinergic vs myasthenic crisis?
back: Edrophonium: improves myasthenic, worsens cholinergic. Clinical: cholinergic = SLUDGE + miosis + bradycardia + fasciculations.

front: Atropine infusion rate?
back: 10-20% of total bolus dose required to achieve endpoints per hour. Titrate to endpoints.

front: OP muscarinic + nicotinic?
back: Muscarinic: SLUDGE + miosis + bradycardia. Nicotinic: fasciculations, weakness, respiratory failure, tachycardia/hypertension.

front: Fasciculations in OP?
back: Nicotinic effect at NMJ. Precedes weakness. Not seen in pure anticholinergic.

front: OP + seizures?
back: Benzos 1st line. Atropine may help (muscarinic). Pralidoxime doesn't cross BBB well.

front: General poisoning management priority?
back: ABCDE first, ALWAYS. Resuscitation before identification.

front: Paracetamol level in ALL overdoses?
back: Yes - co-ingestion common. Paracetamol often with opioids, benzos, alcohol.

front: Activated charcoal dose/timing?
back: 1 g/kg (max 50g), ideally <1h, 1:10 ratio. Benefit up to 2-4h for some (paracetamol, anticholinergics).

front: Osmolal gap formula?
back: Measured - (2×Na + glucose/18 + BUN/2.8 + EtOH/4.6). >10 = toxic alcohol.

front: Digoxin + hyperkalemia?
back: Give Fab FIRST, not insulin/dextrose/calcium. HyperK = poor prognosis (Na/K-ATPase blockade).

front: CO poisoning HBO criteria?
back: COHb >25% (>15% pregnancy), LOC, cardiac ischemia, persistent neuro symptoms. 100% O₂ first.

front: HD indications in poisoning?
back: Methanol, EG, salicylate (severe), lithium, theophylline, metformin, valproate. NOT paracetamol, benzos, opioids.

front: Serotonin vs NMS differentiation?
back: Serotonin: hyperreflexia, clonus, rapid onset (Hunter criteria). NMS: lead-pipe rigidity, bradykinesia, gradual onset (FEVER).

front: Anticholinergic toxidrome mnemonic?
back: Red as a beet, dry as a bone, blind as a bat, mad as a hatter, hot as a hare, full as a flask.

front: Physostigmine in anticholinergic - when?
back: ONLY if severe (seizure, severe agitation, hyperthermia) AND no contraindication (QRS widening, asthma, bradycardia, bowel obstruction).

front: BB vs CCB overdose key difference?
back: BB: Glucagon + HIET. CCB: Calcium + HIET. Both: vasopressors for refractory shock.

front: Poisoning Severity Score (PSS)?
Rumack-Matthew nomogram validity criteria?
back: 4-24h post-ingestion, SINGLE ACUTE ingestion, KNOWN time

front: When is nomogram INVALID? (4 scenarios)?
back: 1) Staggered ingestion 2) Uncertain time 3) >24h post-ingestion 4) Modified-release (SR/ER) 5) ALT elevated at presentation

front: SNAP 2-bag protocol doses and durations?
back: Bag 1: 200 mg/kg over 4h (100mL 5% dextrose). Bag 2: 100 mg/kg over 16h (250mL). Total: 300 mg/kg over 20h. Weight cap 110kg.

front: Anaphylactoid reaction management during NAC?
back: Stop infusion → chlorphenamine 10mg IV + salbutamol neb + fluids → restart at HALF rate once settled. Complete Bag 1 over 4h.

front: Late presentation (>24h) NAC indications?
back: ALT elevated OR paracetamol detectable → FULL NAC COURSE (20h SNAP). If both normal → NAC not needed (confirm assay sensitivity).

front: King's College Criteria (Paracetamol) - ANY ONE?
back: 1) Arterial pH 6.5 + Cr > 300 µmol/L + Grade III/IV encephalopathy

front: INR management in paracetamol ALF?
back: INR = severity marker. Do NOT correct prophylactically. FFP/Vit K ONLY for active bleeding or pre-procedure.

front: Pediatric liquid paracetamol NAC threshold?
back: > 200 mg/kg (or > 150 mg/kg if risk factors: malnutrition, CYP inducers, fasting)

front: Modified-release paracetamol management?
back: Nomogram INVALID. Treat empirically if > 4g ingested. May need extended NAC + repeat levels.

front: NAC timing and survival?
back: 24h: still beneficial if ALT elevated (reduces mortality).

front: Paracetamol level in ALL overdoses - why?
back: Co-ingestion common. Paracetamol often taken with other agents (opioids, benzos, alcohol).

front: Pregnancy + paracetamol overdose - NAC dosing?
back: Same dosing as non-pregnant. NAC safe in pregnancy. Fetal hepatotoxicity rare.

front: High-risk nomogram line?
back: 50 mg/L at 4h (vs standard 100 mg/L). For: alcohol, CYP2E1 inducers (carbamazepine, phenytoin, isoniazid), malnutrition.

front: Hypoglycemia in paracetamol ALF?
back: Monitor glucose q4-6h. Dextrose infusion if needed. Hepatic failure → impaired gluconeogenesis.

front: Weight cap for NAC dosing?
back: Use actual body weight, maximum 110 kg for dose calculation.

front: Classic salicylate acid-base triad?
back: Early: Respiratory alkalosis. Mixed: Respiratory alkalosis + Metabolic acidosis. Late: Metabolic acidosis (respiratory fatigue).

front: Hallmark early symptom?
back: Tinnitus (often first symptom). Hearing loss also common.

front: Urinary alkalinization target urine pH?
back: 7.5-8.0. Check q1-2h with dipstick. Replace K⁺ aggressively (20-40 mEq/L in infusion).

front: Salicylate mechanism of toxicity?
back: 1) Uncouples oxidative phosphorylation → lactic acidosis, hyperthermia. 2) Direct medullary stimulation → hyperventilation, resp alkalosis. 3) Inhibits Krebs cycle. 4) Renal: ion trapping (acid urine ↓ excretion). 5) CNS neurotoxicity.

front: Dialysis criteria - acute vs chronic?
Methyl salicylate (oil of wintergreen) potency?
back: 1 tsp (5mL) = ~7g aspirin. Highly concentrated, small volume = massive dose. Rapid onset, high mortality.

front: Intubation in salicylate poisoning - critical principle?
back: Match pre-intubation minute ventilation (high RR, low TV). Avoid permissive hypercapnia - will worsen acidemia catastrophically.

front: Hypokalemia during alkalinization?
back: Prevents urine alkalinization (K⁺ shifts into cells in exchange for H⁺). Must replace K⁺ to achieve urine pH 7.5-8.0.

front: Done nomogram utility?
back: Limited. Salicylate kinetics complex (saturable metabolism, variable Vd, pH-dependent excretion). Do not rely solely on nomogram.

front: Chronic salicylate toxicity in elderly?
back: Lower serum levels cause severe toxicity. Often missed. Check level in unexplained confusion/acidosis in elderly on chronic aspirin.

front: NCPE management in salicylate?
back: CPAP/BiPAP → intubation + PEEP. NOT fluid overload - avoid fluids/diuretics. Permeability problem.

front: Hyperthermia in salicylate - treatment?
back: Active cooling (evaporative, cold fluids). Antipyretics INEFFECTIVE (salicylate IS antipyretic). Uncoupled OxPhos → heat production.

front: Seizures in salicylate - drug choice?
back: Benzodiazepines 1st line (lorazepam/diazepam). Avoid phenytoin (protein binding displacement, Na channel). Phenobarbital 2nd line.

front: Salicylate level monitoring?
back: Peaks 2-4h (delayed by food, bezoar, pylorospasm). Repeat q2-4h until clearly falling.

front: MUDPILES member - salicylate?
back: Yes - S = Salicylate (anion gap metabolic acidosis). Also lactic acidosis (L) from uncoupled OxPhos.

front: Mechanism of clarithromycin-warfarin interaction?
back: CYP3A4 inhibition (R-warfarin) + CYP2C9 inhibition (S-warfarin) + Mechanism-based inhibition + ↓ Gut Vit K production

front: Warfarin + Fluconazole — management?
back: Reduce warfarin 20-30%, Monitor INR 2-3x/week during + 1 week after

front: Tacrolimus + Clarithromycin — management?
back: Reduce tacrolimus 66-75%, Monitor trough levels q2-3d + Renal function q2-3d

front: Simvastatin + Clarithromycin — management?
back: CONTRAINDICATED. Switch to Rosuvastatin/Pravastatin or hold simvastatin

front: Clopidogrel + Omeprazole — why avoid?
back: Omeprazole = Strong CYP2C19 inhibitor → ↓ Clopidogrel active metabolite → ↑ Stent thrombosis/MI risk

front: P-gp key substrates?
back: Digoxin, Tacrolimus, Ciclosporin, Sirolimus, DOACs, HIV PIs

front: Triple Whammy drugs?
Lithium + Thiazide — monitoring?
back: Li level weekly × 4, then monthly. Target 0.4-0.6 mmol/L. Counsel re: dehydration.

front: Digoxin + Amiodarone — management?
back: Halve digoxin dose. Check level at 1 week (≥6h post-dose). Monitor K⁺, ECG.

front: Phenytoin + Valproate — what level to monitor?
back: FREE phenytoin level (target 1-2 µg/mL). Total level misleading due to displacement.

front: DOAC + Ketoconazole — Dabigatran vs Rivaroxaban vs Apixaban?
back: Dabi: AVOID. Riva: Reduce 15mg OD (CrCl>30). Apix: Reduce 2.5mg BD (if 2/3 criteria).

front: CYP2D6 — any inducers?
Grapefruit juice — which drugs affected?
back: Oral CYP3A4 substrates with HIGH first-pass metabolism (Simvastatin, Felodipine, Tacrolimus). IV drugs unaffected.

front: Rifampicin + Oral Contraceptive — management?
back: Additional barrier contraception during + 4 weeks after stopping rifampicin.

front: Mechanism-based inhibition — onset/offset?
back: Onset: Days (new enzyme synthesis). Offset: Days-weeks (enzyme turnover ~72h).

front: Protein binding displacement — clinically significant for?
back: High extraction, narrow TI drugs: Warfarin, Phenytoin, Valproate.

front: QT prolongation — when to act?
back: QTc >500ms or ΔQTc >60ms from baseline → Stop offending drugs, correct K⁺/Mg²⁺, Cardiology review. Torsades → IV Magnesium 2g bolus, overdrive pacing, isoprenaline. ---

front: What is Diagnostic criteria (ADA/WHO) of Diabetes?
back: FPG ≥7.0 mmol/L · 2h-OGTT ≥11.1 mmol/L · HbA1c ≥48 mmol/mol (6.5%) · Random ≥11.1 + symptoms → confirm with repeat unless unequivocal

front: What is Prediabetes of Diabetes?
How is Diabetes classified?
back: Age, BMI, ketosis, autoantibodies, C-peptide, family history

front: What is Monogenic red flags of Diabetes?
back: Age <25, non-obese, strong FH (3 generations), negative autoantibodies, non-insulin requiring

front: What is the investigation of choice for Diabetes?
back: IADPSG: 75g OGTT → FBG ≥5.1, 1h ≥10.0, 2h ≥8.5 mmol/L · NICE: risk factors + OGTT

front: What is Retinopathy screening of Diabetes?
back: Type 1: 5y post-dx · Type 2: at dx · Annual if no DR · ETDRS grading (10-12 no DR, 20-35 mild/mod NPDR, 43-53 severe NPDR, 61+ PDR)

front: What is DMO of Diabetes?
back: Centre-involving macular oedema · Anti-VEGF 1st line (ranibizumab/aflibercept) · Laser if non-centre

front: What is DKD staging of Diabetes?
back: KDIGO: GFR G1-G5 × ACR A1-A3 (A130 mg/mmol) · Heat map for prognosis

front: How is Diabetes managed?
back: ACEi/ARB max tolerated · SGLT2i (eGFR ≥20) · Finerenone (eGFR ≥25, K+ ≤4.8) · GLP-1 RA

front: What is DSPN of Diabetes?
What is Autonomic neuropathy of Diabetes?
back: CV: resting tachycardia, orthostatic hypotension, silent MI · GI: gastroparesis · GU: ED, bladder

front: What is Foot risk (IWGDF) of Diabetes?
back: 0: no LOPS/PAD · 1: LOPS · 2: LOPS+PAD/deformity · 3: hx ulcer/amputation

front: How is Diabetes classified?
What is Charcot of Diabetes?
back: Eichenholtz 0-3 · Hot swollen foot, preserved pulses · Immobilisation (TCC) · Bisphosphonates?

front: What is MODY red flags of Diabetes?
back: Age <25, non-obese, autosomal dominant (3 generations), negative autoantibodies, C-peptide preserved

front: What is GCK-MODY (MODY2) of Diabetes?
back: Glucokinase defect · Mild fasting hyperglycaemia (5.5-8.0) · No treatment needed · No complications

front: What is HNF1A-MODY (MODY3) of Diabetes?
back: Progressive β-cell defect · Sulfonylurea responsive (excellent response) · Low renal threshold

front: What is HNF4A-MODY (MODY1) of Diabetes?
back: Similar to HNF1A · Macrosomia, neonatal hypoglycaemia · Sulfonylurea responsive

front: What is Neonatal diabetes of Diabetes?
back: <6 months age · KCNJ11 (Kir6.2), ABCC8 (SUR1) = 40-50% · Sulfonylurea responsive (90% switch)

front: What is Transient vs permanent of Diabetes?
back: Transient: remission by 18mo, relapse later · Permanent: lifelong · 6q24 imprinting = transient

front: What is Mitochondrial diabetes of Diabetes?
back: m.3243A>G (MELAS/MIDD) · Maternal inheritance · Sensorineural hearing loss, CPEO, cardiomyopathy

front: What is Wolfram syndrome of Diabetes?
back: WFS1 gene · DIABETES INSIPIDUS, DIABETES MELLITUS, OPTIC ATROPHY, DEAFNESS (DIDMOAD)

front: What is Lipodystrophy of Diabetes?
back: Generalised (CGL) or partial (FPLD) · Severe insulin resistance · Leptin replacement (metreleptin)

front: What is T1D autoimmunity of Diabetes?
back: CD4+/CD8+ T-cell mediated β-cell destruction · Insulitis · GAD65, IA-2, ZnT8, IAA autoantibodies

front: What is T1D genetics of Diabetes?
back: HLA-DR3/DR4-DQ2/DQ8 (40-50% risk) · INS VNTR · PTPN22 · CTLA4 · >50 loci

front: What is T2D insulin resistance of Diabetes?
back: Impaired PI3K-Akt signalling in muscle/liver/adipose · ↑FFA, inflammation (TNF-α, IL-6), adipokines

front: What is T2D β-cell failure of Diabetes?
What is T2D incretin defect of Diabetes?
What is T2D genetics of Diabetes?
back: TCF7L2 (strongest) · KCNJ11 · PPARG · FTO · >400 loci (polygenic risk score)

front: What is GDM screening of Diabetes?
back: IADPSG: universal 75g OGTT 24-28wk (FBG≥5.1, 1h≥10.0, 2h≥8.5) · NICE: risk factors then OGTT · ADA: universal or risk-based

front: What is GDM targets of Diabetes?
back: FBG <5.3, 1h <7.8, 2h <6.4 mmol/L · HbA1c not recommended for monitoring

front: How is Diabetes managed?

front: What is the definition of Injectable non insulin therapy?

front: What is King's College Criteria (Paracetamol) of Acute Liver Failure?
back: pH 100s (INR >6.5) + Cr >300 µmol/L + Grade III/IV HE

front: What is King's College Criteria (Non-paracetamol) of Acute Liver Failure?
back: PT >100s (INR >6.5) OR ANY THREE: Age 40 + Aetiology (non-A, non-B hepatitis, halothane, idiosyncratic drugs) + Jaundice to encephalopathy >7 days + PT >50s (INR >3.5) + Bilirubin >300 µmol/L

front: What is CLIF-C ACLF Score of Acute Liver Failure?
back: Organ failures: Liver (bil >12), Kidney (Cr >2 or RRT), Brain (HE grade III/IV), Coagulation (INR >2.5), Circulation (vasopressors), Respiration (PaO2/FiO2 <200 or SpO2/FiO2 <214 or MV)

front: What is ACLF Grades of Acute Liver Failure?
back: Grade 1: 1 OF (kidney) or 1 OF (non-kidney) + Cr 1.5-1.9 or HE I-II; Grade 2: 2 OFs; Grade 3: ≥3 OFs

front: What is Paracetamol NAC of Acute Liver Failure?
back: IV 3-bag regimen: 150mg/kg/1h → 50mg/kg/4h → 100mg/kg/16h (total 300mg/kg/21h); continue if INR improving/hepatic encephalopathy

front: What is the definition of Acute Liver Failure?

front: What causes Acute Liver Failure?

back: Grade 1: 1 OF (kidney) or 1 OF + Cr 1.5-1.9/HE I-II; Grade 2: 2 OFs; Grade 3: ≥3 OFs

front: What is the definition of Viral Hepatitis?

back: Dull, aching, deep, worse at night, mechanical (worse with weight-bearing), unrelieved by rest

front: What is Deformity of Bone Metabolic Diseases?
back: Bowing of femur/tibia (saber shin), enlarged skull (hat size ↑), kyphosis, waddling gait

front: What is Fractures of Bone Metabolic Diseases?
back: Insufficiency fractures — transverse (chalkstick) in long bones, vertebral

front: What is Nerve Compression of Bone Metabolic Diseases?
back: Basilar invagination → cranial nerve palsies, spinal stenosis → radiculopathy, deafness (VIIIth nerve)

front: What is Joint Involvement of Bone Metabolic Diseases?
back: Secondary OA adjacent to pagetic bone (hip, knee)

front: What is High-Output Cardiac Failure of Bone Metabolic Diseases?
back: Vascular steal from highly vascular pagetic bone (rare, extensive disease)

front: What is Demographics of Bone Metabolic Diseases?
back: >55y, M>F, UK highest, SQSTM1 mutation (20-30% familial)

front: What is Three Phases of Bone Metabolic Diseases?
back: 1) Osteolytic (ALP↑↑, lytic wedge) → 2) Mixed (ALP↑↑↑, cotton wool) → 3) Sclerotic (ALP normal, ivory vertebra)

front: What is ALP of Bone Metabolic Diseases?
back: Markedly elevated (bone isoform) — diagnosis + monitoring; normal in sclerotic phase

front: What is Bone Scan of Bone Metabolic Diseases?
What is Common Sites of Bone Metabolic Diseases?
back: Pelvis > Lumbar spine > Femur > Thoracic spine > Skull

front: How is Bone Metabolic Diseases managed?
back: Zoledronate 5mg IV single dose (1st line, 90%+ remission); oral risedronate/alendronate if IV not possible

front: What is Bone Metabolic Diseases indicated for?
back: Pain, high-risk site (skull base, spine), pre-op, nerve compression

front: What are the complications of Bone Metabolic Diseases?
back: Fractures, OA, nerve compression (deafness), osteosarcoma (1%), high-output HF

front: What is Osteosarcoma of Bone Metabolic Diseases?
back: ~1%; pain worsening, ALP rising despite Rx; biopsy mandatory

front: What is the definition of Clinical Approach to Musculoskeletal Disease?
back: # Bone Structure & Remodelling and Joint Biology

front: How is Clinical Approach to Musculoskeletal Disease classified?
back: Cortical 80% mass (Haversian), trabecular 20% mass, 80% surface (metabolically active)

front: What is Bone cells of Clinical Approach to Musculoskeletal Disease?
back: Osteoclast (resorption, monocyte origin); osteoblast (formation, MSC origin); osteocyte (mechanosensor, sclerostin/RANKL)

front: What is BMU of Clinical Approach to Musculoskeletal Disease?
back: Activation → Resorption (2-3wk) → Reversal → Formation (3-4mo) → Mineralisation → Quiescence

front: What is RANKL of Clinical Approach to Musculoskeletal Disease?
back: From osteoblasts/osteocytes/T cells → osteoclastogenesis; OPG = decoy receptor

front: What is Denosumab of Clinical Approach to Musculoskeletal Disease?
back: Anti-RANKL mAb → osteoclast inhibition; stopping causes rebound (need bisphosphonate cover)

front: What is Wnt/sclerostin of Clinical Approach to Musculoskeletal Disease?
back: Wnt → bone formation; sclerostin (from osteocytes) blocks Wnt; mechanical loading ↓ sclerostin

front: What is Romosozumab of Clinical Approach to Musculoskeletal Disease?
back: Anti-sclerostin → anabolic + anti-resorptive; boxed warning MACE

front: What is PTH of Clinical Approach to Musculoskeletal Disease?
back: Continuous high = resorption; intermittent low = formation (teriparatide)

front: What is Glucocorticoids of Clinical Approach to Musculoskeletal Disease?
back: Severe bone loss: ↓ osteoblast, ↑ osteoblast apoptosis, ↑ RANKL, ↓ Ca²⁺ absorption, ↓ sex hormones

front: What is Articular cartilage of Clinical Approach to Musculoskeletal Disease?
back: Type II collagen + aggrecan + water (70-80%); chondrocytes <5%

front: What is Cartilage degradation of Clinical Approach to Musculoskeletal Disease?
back: MMPs (collagen), aggrecanases (ADAMTS-4, -5) (aggrecan); no effective DMOAD yet

front: What is Synovial fluid analysis of Clinical Approach to Musculoskeletal Disease?
back: Septic: WBC >50,000, PMN >75%, culture +ve; aspirate before antibiotics

front: What is RA erosion of Clinical Approach to Musculoskeletal Disease?
back: Synovial cytokines (TNF, IL-1, IL-6) → RANKL on FLS + osteoblasts → osteoclast activation

front: What is Pannus of Clinical Approach to Musculoskeletal Disease?
back: Invasive synovial tissue; FLS + macrophages + T cells + B cells; drives erosion in RA

front: What is Mechanical vs inflammatory enthesitis of Clinical Approach to Musculoskeletal Disease?
back: SpA: insertional + bone oedema, IL-23/17; Mechanical: degenerative, eccentric exercise

front: What is Bone loss in RA of Clinical Approach to Musculoskeletal Disease?
back: Inflammation + RANKL + ACPA; treat with DMARDs/biologics; denosumab reduces erosion

front: What is the definition of Crystal Arthropathies?
back: Deposition of calcium pyrophosphate dihydrate (CPPD) crystals in articular cartilage, fibrocartilage, and soft tissues → acute inflammation ("pseudogout") or chronic arthropathy

front: What is the epidemiology of Crystal Arthropathies?
What is Peak Onset of Crystal Arthropathies?
What is Sex Ratio of Crystal Arthropathies?
back: M = F (slight female predominance in elderly)

front: What is Genetics of Crystal Arthropathies?
back: ANKH mutations (familial CPPD); sporadic more common

front: What is Crystal of Crystal Arthropathies?
back: CPPD = Rhomboid/Rectangular, Weak Positive Birefringence (Blue Parallel)

front: What is Mnemonic of Crystal Arthropathies?
back: Pseudogout = Blue Rhomboids (Positive) vs Gout = Yellow Needles (Negative)

front: What is Chondrocalcinosis of Crystal Arthropathies?
back: Linear calcification of fibrocartilage: Menisci (knee), TFCC (wrist), Pubic symphysis

front: What is Clinical of Crystal Arthropathies?
back: Knee > Wrist > Ankle; older (>65); less dramatic than gout

front: What is Associations (Screen!) of Crystal Arthropathies?
back: Haemochromatosis (Ferritin, TSAT), Hyperparathyroidism (PTH, Ca), Hypomagnesaemia (Mg), Hypophosphatasia (low ALP), ANKH (familial)

front: What is Crowned Dens of Crystal Arthropathies?
back: CPPD in atlantoaxial → acute neck pain, fever, periodontoid calcification on CT

front: What is Acute Rx of Crystal Arthropathies?
back: NSAID / Colchicine low-dose / IA Steroid / Oral Pred (like gout)

front: What is No ULT of Crystal Arthropathies?
back: No allopurinol equivalent — treat underlying cause only

front: What is Chronic Arthropathy of Crystal Arthropathies?
What is the definition of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: JIA = most common chronic arthritis in children (<16y).

front: What is Joints of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: 1-4 in first 6m; asymmetric large joints (knee > ankle > elbow)

front: What is Uveitis Risk of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: Chronic anterior uveitis — ANA+ = high risk (20-30%); asymptomatic, insidious

front: What is Screening of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: Slit-lamp every 3 months (ANA+), every 6 months (ANA-) for 7y, then annually

front: What is Leg Length Discrepancy of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: Asymmetric growth → hemihypertrophy → limb length inequality

front: What is Extended Oligo of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: >4 joints after 6m → higher uveitis risk, joint damage

front: What is Trigger of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: Infection, flare, drug change (esp. in sJIA)

front: What is the investigation of choice for Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: Ferritin >10,000 + ≥2 cytopenias + hypertriglyceridaemia + hypofibrinogenaemia + high LDH + CNS dysfunction + hemophagocytosis (bone marrow)

front: How is Inflammatory Arthritis managed?
back: IV MP 10-30mg/kg/day ×3-5d + Anakinra 4-10mg/kg/day IV/SC + IVIG + Ciclosporin A + Etoposide (refractory)

front: What is Timing of Inflammatory Arthritis?
What is Key Elements of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: Disease education, self-management, medication adherence, reproductive health, vocational planning, adult rheumatology handover

front: What is Transition Coordinator of Inflammatory Arthritis?
What is Adult Rheumatology of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: Continued uveitis screening, bone health, cardiovascular risk

front: How is Inflammatory Arthritis classified?
back: Systemic, Oligo (persistent/extended), Poly RF-, Poly RF+, Psoriatic, Enthesitis-related, Undifferentiated

front: What is Systemic JIA of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: Quotidian fever + salmon rash + arthritis = classic; IL-1/IL-6 driven; MAS risk (ferritin >10k)

front: What is Oligoarticular of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: <6y, 1-4 joints, asymmetric knee/ankle, ANA+ = high uveitis risk (screen q3mo)

front: What is Polyarticular RF+ of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: Adolescent, mimics adult RA, RF/anti-CCP+, erosive, MTX → anti-TNF

front: What is Enthesitis-related of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: HLA-B27+, M>F, >6y, enthesitis, sacroiliitis, acute uveitis

front: What is Psoriatic JIA of Inflammatory Arthritis?
What is Uveitis Screening of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: ANA+ oligo = q3mo; ANA- = q6mo; chronic uveitis = asymptomatic

front: What is MAS of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: Ferritin >10,000 + cytopenias + coagulopathy → IV MP + Anakinra (IL-1Ra)

front: What is Transition of Inflammatory Arthritis?
What is Common themes of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: Enthesitis, sacroiliitis, dactylitis, uveitis, psoriasis, IBD, family history

front: What is Seronegative of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: RF negative, anti-CCP negative (anti-CCP differentiates from RA)

front: What is Spectrum of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: Ankylosing spondylitis (AS), Psoriatic arthritis (PsA), Reactive arthritis (ReA), Enteropathic arthritis, Undifferentiated SpA, Juvenile SpA

front: What is HLA-B27 of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: Class I MHC; misfolded protein → IL-23 production → Th17 activation; population prevalence 8% (Western); >90% AS; 20% of carriers develop SpA

front: What is IL-23/IL-17 axis of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: Master cytokine pathway in SpA; target of ustekinumab (anti-IL-12/23), risankizumab (anti-IL-23), secukinumab/ixekizumab (anti-IL-17)

front: What is Enthesitis of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: Insertion of tendon/ligament into bone; IL-23-expressing cells at enthesis → IL-17 → inflammation + bone oedema

front: What is Gut-joint axis of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: Subclinical gut inflammation in 60% of SpA; IBD increases SpA risk; HLA-B27+ rats develop both gut and joint disease

front: What is Mucosal immunity of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: ReA follows GU/GI infection; PsA follows psoriasis; common mucosal immune dysregulation

front: What is Inflammatory back pain of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: Age 3 months, improves with exercise, worse with rest, night pain, AM stiffness >30 min

front: What is Sacroiliitis of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: Buttock pain (often alternating), positive FABER / Patrick's test, Mennel, direct pressure; imaging: X-ray (subchondral sclerosis, erosions, ankylosis) or MRI (bone marrow oedema)

front: What is Enthesitis of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: Achilles, plantar fascia, lateral epicondyle, costochondral; MASES index (Maastricht Ankylosing Spondylitis Enthesitis Score)

front: What is Dactylitis of Inflammatory Arthritis?
What is Uveitis of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: Acute anterior uveitis (AAU); HLA-B27+; unilateral, painful, photophobic, red eye — emergency ophthalmology

front: What is Psoriasis of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: Skin (often occult) or nail changes (pitting, onycholysis)

front: What is IBD of Inflammatory Arthritis?
What is Family history of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: SpA, psoriasis, IBD, AAU in 1st-degree relatives

front: What is Synovitis pattern of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: Asymmetrical, oligoarticular, lower limb predominant (vs RA: symmetrical, small joints)

front: What is Axial skeleton of Inflammatory Arthritis?
What is ESR, CRP of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: Inflammatory markers (may be normal despite active disease)

front: What is FBC of Inflammatory Arthritis?
What is HLA-B27 of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: Supportive; high pre-test probability needed (positive in 8% normal population)

front: What is RF, anti-CCP of Inflammatory Arthritis?
What is U&E, LFT of Inflammatory Arthritis?
What is Urinalysis of Inflammatory Arthritis?
What is CXR of Inflammatory Arthritis?
What is Pelvis X-ray (AP) of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: Sacroiliitis (modified New York criteria); spine X-ray (bamboo spine)

front: What is MRI SI joints of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: Gold standard for early sacroiliitis (bone marrow oedema, enthesitis) — when X-ray normal

front: What is Ultrasound of Inflammatory Arthritis?
What is Clinical of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: Low back pain ≥3 months, improved by exercise, not relieved by rest; limitation of lumbar spine; limitation of chest expansion

front: What is Radiographic of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: Bilateral sacroiliitis grade ≥2 OR unilateral sacroiliitis grade ≥3-4

front: What is Definite AS of Inflammatory Arthritis?
What is Method of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: Flow cytometry or PCR; not a "screening" test

front: What is the investigation of choice for Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: Symptomatic patient with inflammatory back pain → post-test probability high

front: What is the epidemiology of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: 8% in West; up to 50% in Haida, indigenous populations

front: How is Inflammatory Arthritis classified?
back: AS 90%, ReA 60-80%, PsA axial 20-50%, IBD 30-50%, acute anterior uveitis 50%

front: What is Positive in healthy of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: 8% → lifetime SpA risk 2-5%; HLA-B27 alone does not diagnose SpA

front: What is Negative result of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: Does not exclude SpA (10-20% of AS, 50% of PsA are HLA-B27−)

front: What is the epidemiology of Inflammatory Arthritis?
What is Clinical of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: Unilateral, painful, red eye, photophobia, blurred vision, miosis; acute onset

front: What is Pathology of Inflammatory Arthritis?
What is Course of Inflammatory Arthritis?
What are the complications of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: Posterior synechiae, cataract, glaucoma, macular oedema

front: How is Inflammatory Arthritis managed?
back: Emergency ophthalmology; topical steroid + cycloplegic; systemic steroid for severe; anti-TNF (especially adalimumab) reduces recurrence

front: What is Differential of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: Conjunctivitis (no pain, no photophobia), episcleritis, scleritis, keratitis

front: What are the clinical features of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: HLA-B27, enthesitis, dactylitis, sacroiliitis, uveitis, psoriasis, IBD, family hx, seronegative

front: What is Inflammatory back pain of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: 3 months, improves with exercise, worse with rest, night pain, AM stiffness >30 min

front: What is ASAS axial SpA of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: Back pain ≥3 months, <45y: imaging arm (sacroiliitis on MRI/X-ray + ≥1 feature) OR clinical arm (HLA-B27 + ≥2 features)

front: What is ASAS peripheral SpA of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: Arthritis OR enthesitis OR dactylitis + ≥1 feature (OR all 3 + ≥2 features)

front: What is HLA-B27 of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: 90% AS, 60-80% ReA, 20-50% PsA, 30-50% IBD; don't screen in non-specific back pain

front: What is CASPAR for PsA of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: Current psoriasis +2, hx psoriasis +1, nail dystrophy +1, RF neg +1, dactylitis +1, juxta-articular new bone +1; ≥3 = PsA

front: What is AS mNY criteria of Inflammatory Arthritis?
What is MRI vs X-ray of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: MRI for early active inflammation (bone marrow oedema); X-ray for late structural damage (years)

front: What is Uveitis of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: Acute anterior uveitis; HLA-B27; unilateral, painful, red; emergency; adalimumab reduces recurrence

front: What is the first-line treatment for Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: Naproxen 500 BD or celecoxib 200 BD; continuous for axial SpA; GI + CV protection

front: What is Biologics (axial) of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: Anti-TNF (ADA, IFX, ETA, CZP, GOL) or anti-IL-17 (SEC, IXE, BIM); csDMARDs no efficacy in axial

front: What is Anti-TNF for uveitis/IBD of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: Adalimumab (best for both); etanercept does NOT work for IBD/uveitis (decoy receptor)

front: What is JAKi of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: Tofacitinib (PsA), upadacitinib (AS, PsA); boxed warning MACE/VTE/malignancy

front: What is Pregnancy safe of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: NSAIDs (1st/2nd tri), paracetamol, certolizumab, sulfasalazine; avoid MTX, LEF

front: What are the complications of Inflammatory Arthritis?
back: AA amyloidosis (chronic inflammation), AAU (uveitis), aortitis (AS), CVD (↑ risk), fractures (osteoporosis)

front: What is Wind-Up of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: Progressive increase in dorsal horn neuron response to repeated C-fibre stimulation

front: What is Temporal Summation of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: Increasing pain perception with repeated identical stimuli

front: What is Allodynia of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: Pain from non-painful stimulus (light touch, brushing)

front: What is Hyperalgesia of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: Exaggerated pain response to painful stimulus

front: What is Loss of Descending Inhibition of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
What is Impaired CPM of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
What is the definition of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: Chronic pain ≥4/5 body regions (axial + 3 quadrants) ≥3 months

front: What is vs Fibromyalgia of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: CWP = broader; FM = CWP + ACR 2016 (WPI ≥7 & SSS ≥5 OR WPI 4-6 & SSS ≥9)

front: What is Central Sensitisation of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: Wind-up, temporal summation, allodynia, hyperalgesia, loss of descending inhibition, impaired CPM

front: What are the clinical features of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: Pain (widespread) + Fatigue + Unrefreshing sleep + Cognitive dysfunction ("brain fog")

front: What is Comorbidities of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: IBS, CFS, migraine, TMJ, depression/anxiety, interstitial cystitis, RLS

front: What is the investigation of choice for Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: Clinical (definition + exclusion); no confirmatory test, normal ESR/CRP/CK

front: How is Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes managed?
What is Drugs of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: Duloxetine, Pregabalin, Amitriptyline (low dose) — NO opioids, benzos, muscle relaxants

front: What is Multidisciplinary of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
What is the definition of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: Tendinopathy of common extensor origin (ECRB most common) at lateral epicondyle

front: What is the epidemiology of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: 1-3% population, 30-50 years, manual workers, tennis players

front: What is Pathology of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
What is Pain of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: Lateral elbow, radiates to forearm, worse with gripping/lifting

front: What is Tenderness of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
What is the investigation of choice for Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: Cozen's test, Maudsley's test, Mill's test, Chair test

front: What is the epidemiology of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: Less common than lateral, golfers, throwers, manual workers

front: What is Pain of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: Medial elbow, radiates to forearm, worse with gripping/flexion

front: What is Tenderness of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
What is the investigation of choice for Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: Resisted wrist flexion, resisted pronation, golf swing test

front: What is the definition of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: Compression of PIN (Posterior Interosseous Nerve) in radial tunnel (arcade of Frohse)

front: What is Key Differentiator from Tennis Elbow of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: NO MOTOR DEFICIT (PIN injury = wrist/finger drop); pain only

front: What is Clinical of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: Deep aching dorsal forearm pain, resisted middle finger extension (pain), resisted supination (pain)

front: What is Exam of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: Tenderness 4-5cm distal to lateral epicondyle (over radial tunnel), NO weakness

front: What is the investigation of choice for Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: Resisted middle finger extension (pain in radial tunnel), resisted supination

front: What is Differential of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: Tennis elbow: lateral epicondyle tenderness, Cozen's/Maudsley +ve; Radial tunnel: deep forearm pain, motor intact

front: What is the definition of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: Ulnar nerve compression at cubital tunnel (retrocondylar groove)

front: What causes Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: Prolonged elbow flexion, trauma, arthritis, cubitus valgus

front: What is Clinical of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: Numbness/tingling ulnar 1.5 digits, Tinel's at elbow, worsens with elbow flexion, nocturnal symptoms

front: What is Motor (Late) of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: Thenar wasting, Froment's sign +ve (adductor pollicis weakness), weakness finger abduction/adduction, claw hand

front: What is Exam of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: Tinel's at elbow +ve, elbow flexion test +ve, Froment's sign (paper pull → thumb IP flexion)

front: What is the definition of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: Median nerve compression at pronator teres (between two heads)

front: What is Clinical of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: Proximal forearm pain, numbness median nerve distribution (sparing thenar — AIN variant), weakness pronation, thumb opposition

front: What is Exam of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: Tinel's at proximal forearm, resisted pronation painful/weak, PT tenderness

front: What is AIN Syndrome of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: Pure motor (branch to FPL, FDP index, pronator quadratus) — thenar wasting, pinch weakness, "OK sign" abnormal

front: What is the mechanism of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
What is Clinical of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: Lateral elbow pain, limited pronation/supination, swelling

front: What is X-ray of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: Fat pad sign (anterior sail, posterior = fracture), radial head fracture line

front: How is Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes classified?
back: I: non-displaced; II: displaced >2mm; III: comminuted

front: What is Tennis Elbow of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: Lateral epicondylitis, Cozen's test (resisted wrist extension + radial deviation), Maudsley (resisted middle finger extension)

front: What is Golfer's Elbow of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: Medial epicondylitis, resisted wrist flexion/pronation, medial epicondyle tenderness

front: What is Olecranon Bursitis of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: Fluctuant posterior swelling, aspirate if hot/erythematous (septic/gout/RA/CPPD)

front: What is Radial Tunnel Syndrome of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: Deep dorsal forearm pain, resisted middle finger extension, NO motor deficit (vs PIN palsy)

front: What is Cubital Tunnel of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: Ulnar nerve at elbow, Tinel's at elbow, numbness ulnar 1.5 digits, Froment's sign

front: What is Pronator Teres of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: Median nerve at pronator teres, proximal forearm pain, Tinel's proximal forearm

front: What is Emergency of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: Septic olecranon bursitis → aspirate, IV antibiotics, surgical drainage

front: What is X-ray of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: Fat pad sign (posterior = radial head fracture), Mason classification for radial head fractures

front: What is Wind-Up of Osteoarthritis and Related Disorders?
back: Progressive increase in dorsal horn neuron response to repeated C-fibre stimulation

front: What is Temporal Summation of Osteoarthritis and Related Disorders?
back: Increasing pain perception with repeated identical stimuli

front: What is Allodynia of Osteoarthritis and Related Disorders?
back: Pain from non-painful stimulus (light touch, brushing)

front: What is Hyperalgesia of Osteoarthritis and Related Disorders?
back: Exaggerated pain response to painful stimulus

front: What is Loss of Descending Inhibition of Osteoarthritis and Related Disorders?
What is Impaired CPM of Osteoarthritis and Related Disorders?
What is the definition of Osteoarthritis and Related Disorders?
back: Chronic widespread pain + central sensitisation — no structural pathology

front: What is ACR 2016 Criteria of Osteoarthritis and Related Disorders?
back: WPI ≥7 & SSS ≥5 OR WPI 4-6 & SSS ≥9 (symptoms ≥3 months) — no tender points needed

front: What is Central Sensitisation of Osteoarthritis and Related Disorders?
back: Wind-up, temporal summation, allodynia, hyperalgesia, loss of descending inhibition

front: What are the clinical features of Osteoarthritis and Related Disorders?
back: Pain (widespread) + Fatigue + Unrefreshing sleep + Cognitive dysfunction ("fibro fog")

front: What is Comorbidities of Osteoarthritis and Related Disorders?
back: IBS, CFS, migraine, TMJ, depression/anxiety, interstitial cystitis, RLS

front: What is the investigation of choice for Osteoarthritis and Related Disorders?
back: Clinical (ACR 2016 criteria + exclusion); no confirmatory test, normal ESR/CRP/CK

front: How is Osteoarthritis and Related Disorders managed?
What is Drugs of Osteoarthritis and Related Disorders?
back: Duloxetine (SNRI), Pregabalin (α2δ), Amitriptyline (low dose) — NO opioids, benzodiazepines

front: What is Multidisciplinary of Osteoarthritis and Related Disorders?
What is Location of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
What is Risk of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: Kneeling occupations (carpet layers, plumbers, roofers, gardeners)

front: What is Clinical of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: Anterior swelling, warm, tender, ROM usually preserved

front: What is Septic of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
What is Imaging of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: US: fluid collection anterior to patella

front: What is Anatomy of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: Confluence of Sartorius, Gracilis, Semitendinosus (Pes anserinus = "goose foot")

front: What is Location of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
What causes Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: Obesity, OA knee, diabetes, overuse, valgus deformity

front: What is Clinical of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: Medial knee pain, tenderness 2-3cm below joint line (medial), pain on stairs, night pain

front: What is Differential of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: Medial meniscal tear (joint line tenderness), MCL sprain, medial compartment OA

front: What is the definition of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: Synovial fluid-filled cyst in popliteal fossa, communicates with knee joint via valving mechanism

front: What causes Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: Underlying knee pathology: OA (most common), RA, meniscal tears, ACL tears

front: What is the mechanism of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: Valving mechanism — fluid enters cyst during knee extension, trapped during flexion

front: What is Rupture of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: Pseudothrombophlebitis — calf swelling, pain, erythema, Homan's sign +ve, mimics DVT

front: What is Imaging of Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes?
back: US/MRI: fluid collection in popliteal fossa, communication with joint

front: How is Soft Tissue Rheumatism and Chronic Pain Syndromes managed?

front: What imaging is essential to confirm thrombectomy eligibility?

front: What must be avoided in diabetes treatment after stroke?

front: What pathway does SSEP mainly assess?
back: Posterior column-medial lemniscus sensory pathway to cortex.

front: What does BAEP assess?
back: Auditory nerve and brainstem auditory conduction pathways.

front: What does prolonged latency imply?
back: Slowed conduction, often due to demyelination.

front: Do evoked potentials replace MRI?
back: No, they complement MRI and clinical localization.

front: What can reduce SSEP amplitude besides central disease?
What does VEP assess?
back: Functional conduction in the visual pathway.

front: Classic VEP abnormality in optic neuritis?
Classic chapter association for VEP?
What can reduced amplitude suggest?
back: More severe dysfunction or axonal damage.

front: What exam pair should you remember?
What bedside signs support optic neuritis?
back: Painful visual loss, color desaturation, RAPD.

front: Best one-line principle?
back: VEP delay means slowed visual pathway conduction, often demyelination.

front: What is the classic routine indication for EEG after a seizure?
When is urgent EEG needed?
back: When non-convulsive status epilepticus is suspected.

front: Why request EEG in recurrent stereotyped episodes?
back: To support epileptic tendency and help classify the disorder.

front: What is a common pitfall with EEG?
back: Over-requesting it for clearly non-epileptic events.

front: What does EEG mainly add after first unprovoked seizure?
What is the key principle for EEG use?
back: Request it selectively when epilepsy is clinically plausible.

front: What is the commonest canal involved in BPPV?
Typical duration of a BPPV attack?
back: Seconds, usually less than a minute.

front: Best bedside test for posterior canal BPPV?
Best treatment for posterior canal BPPV?
Main pathophysiology of BPPV?
back: Displaced utricular otoconia within a semicircular canal.

front: Does simple BPPV usually cause hearing loss?
back: No, not as a defining feature.

front: Name one major central red flag in vertigo assessment.?
back: Diplopia, dysarthria, limb ataxia, or non-fatigable vertical nystagmus.

front: What test is used for suspected horizontal canal BPPV?
Are vestibular suppressants definitive therapy for BPPV?
back: No, repositioning maneuvers are the main treatment.

front: What lifestyle issue matters especially in older BPPV patients?
Which nystagmus patterns strongly suggest central vertigo?
What does skew deviation imply?
back: Central pathology affecting ocular alignment pathways.

front: What gait clue is especially worrying in vertigo?
back: Inability to sit or stand unsupported due to severe truncal ataxia.

front: What associated symptoms strongly support a central cause?
back: Diplopia, dysarthria, dysphagia, facial numbness, limb weakness, or sensory changes.

front: Does severe vomiting alone distinguish central from peripheral vertigo?
back: No, associated focal signs and exam pattern matter more.

front: What bedside framework may help in acute vestibular syndrome?
What is the safest rule in suspected central vertigo?
back: If the neurological exam is abnormal, think central and image urgently.

front: Why is central vertigo dangerous to miss?
back: It may reflect serious brainstem, cerebellar, inflammatory, infective, or mass lesions.

front: Nystagmus is named by which phase?
What peripheral vestibular nystagmus pattern is most typical?
Name 3 central warning nystagmus patterns.?
back: Vertical, pure torsional, and direction-changing gaze-evoked nystagmus.

front: What positional nystagmus pattern is classic for posterior canal BPPV?
What effect does fixation often have on peripheral vestibular nystagmus?
What associated sign makes atypical nystagmus more dangerous?
back: Severe truncal ataxia or focal neurological signs.

front: Why is nystagmus useful in vertigo?
back: It helps localize peripheral versus central disease.

front: What is an exam trap with positional nystagmus?
back: Assuming all positional nystagmus is benign BPPV.

front: What is Alexander law in simple terms?
back: Peripheral nystagmus often becomes more obvious when gazing in the fast-phase direction.

front: What should always accompany nystagmus interpretation?

front: What is the definition of Clinical Immunology?
back: ## 🧠 Core Concept: Secondary vs Primary Immunodeficiency

front: What is Neutrophil dysfunction (Chemotaxis, Phagocytosis, ROS) of Clinical Immunology?
back: S. aureus (Skin/soft tissue, Osteomyelitis), Fungal (Candidiasis, Mucormucosis), UTI (E. coli, Klebsiella), TB (↑3x)

front: What is Hyperglycaemia of Clinical Immunology?
back: Impaired neutrophil function, Impaired T cell function

front: What is Neuropathy/Vascular disease of Clinical Immunology?
What is the definition of Clinical Immunology?
back: A[Loss of Self-Tolerance] --> B[Central Tolerance FailureThymus: Negative selection defect]

front: What is Clonal Deletion of Clinical Immunology?
What is Treg Differentiation of Clinical Immunology?
What is Receptor Editing of Clinical Immunology?
back: B cells: Light chain rearrangement (κ/λ) → Altered specificity

front: What is Joints of Clinical Immunology?
back: Symmetrical polyarthritis (MCP, PIP, Wrist), Morning stiffness >1h, Synovitis

front: What is Extra-articular of Clinical Immunology?
back: Rheumatoid nodules, Rheumatoid vasculitis, Felty's syndrome, Interstitial lung disease, Pericarditis, Amyloidosis

front: What is Systemic of Clinical Immunology?
back: Fatigue, Low-grade fever, Weight loss, Anaemia of chronic disease

front: What is MTX of Clinical Immunology?
back: CBC, LFT, Renal function q8-12w; CXR baseline

front: What is Leflunomide of Clinical Immunology?
What is Anti-TNF of Clinical Immunology?
back: TB screen (IGRA/TST) pre-tx; HBV/HIV screen; LFT, CBC q3-6m

front: What is Rituximab of Clinical Immunology?
back: IgG, CD19+ count pre-infusion; Hepatitis B screen

front: What is JAKi of Clinical Immunology?
back: CBC, LFT, Lipids, CK, VZV IgG; TB screen; CV risk assessment

front: What is Enthesitis of Clinical Immunology?
back: SPARCC Enthesitis Index (6 sites), LEI (Leeds Enthesitis Index), MASSE

front: What is Dactylitis of Clinical Immunology?
back: LDI (Leeds Dactylitis Index) — Tender + Swollen digit count

front: What is Autoantibody of Clinical Immunology?
What is Clinical of Clinical Immunology?
back: Hyperthyroidism (Weight loss, Tremor, Tachycardia, Heat intolerance), Goitre, Thyroid Eye Disease (TED), Pretibial myxoedema, Acropachy

front: What is TED of Clinical Immunology?
back: Proptosis, Diplopia, Exposure keratopathy, Optic neuropathy — CAS/Activity scoring (EUROGOON)

front: What is the investigation of choice for Clinical Immunology?
back: Low TSH, High FT4/FT3, TRAb+ (Diagnostic), TSHR Ab

front: How is Clinical Immunology managed?
back: Antithyroid drugs (Carbimazole/Methimazole — 1st line), Radioiodine (I-131), Thyroidectomy (Large goitre, TED, Pregnancy)

front: What is Autoantibodies of Clinical Immunology?
What is Clinical of Clinical Immunology?
back: Goitre (Firm, Non-tender), Hypothyroidism (Fatigue, Weight gain, Cold intolerance), Associated: Coeliac, T1DM, APS, Vitiligo, Pernicious anaemia

front: What is the investigation of choice for Clinical Immunology?
How is Clinical Immunology managed?
back: Levothyroxine (1.6 μg/kg/d), Target TSH 0.5-2.5 mU/L; Monitor TSH 6-8w after dose change

front: What is Criteria of Clinical Immunology?
back: IAIHG Simplified Score (≥6 Probable, ≥7 Definite): ANA/SMA ≥1:40, IgG >ULN, Histology, Exclusion viral

front: What is Autoantibodies of Clinical Immunology?
What is Histology of Clinical Immunology?
back: Interface hepatitis, Plasma cells, Rosettes, Emperor cells

front: How is Clinical Immunology managed?
What is Relapse of Clinical Immunology?
What is Transplant of Clinical Immunology?
back: Fulminant hepatic failure, Decompensated cirrhosis unresponsive

front: What is Criteria of Clinical Immunology?
back: 2/3: ALP >1.5x ULN, AMA+ (≥1:40), Histology (Florid duct lesion)

front: What is Antibody of Clinical Immunology?
What is Staging of Clinical Immunology?
How is Clinical Immunology managed?
back: Ursodeoxycholic Acid (UDCA) 13-15mg/kg/d — Biochemical response at 12m (Paris I/II/Toronto criteria)

front: What is Second-Line of Clinical Immunology?
What are the complications of Clinical Immunology?
back: Portal HTN, Osteoporosis, Fat-soluble vitamin deficiency, HCC (Cirrhosis)

front: What is Association of Clinical Immunology?
What is the investigation of choice for Clinical Immunology?
What is Biomarkers of Clinical Immunology?
How is Clinical Immunology managed?
What is Cancer Risk of Clinical Immunology?
What is Transplant of Clinical Immunology?
back: Only curative option for decompensated cirrhosis

front: What is Criteria of Clinical Immunology?
back: 1) Clinical/Radiological (Organ involvement), 2) Serum IgG4 >1.35g/L, 3) Histology (Dense lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate, IgG4+ plasma cells >10/HPF, IgG4/IgG >40%, Storiform fibrosis, Obliterative phlebitis)

front: What is Organ Involvement of Clinical Immunology?
back: Pancreas (Type 1 AIP), Salivary glands (Mikulicz), Bile duct (Sclerosing cholangitis), Retroperitoneum, Kidney (Tubulointerstitial nephritis), Lung, Lacrimal, Thyroid (Riedel's)

front: How is Clinical Immunology managed?
What is Fatigable Weakness of Clinical Immunology?
back: Proximal > Distal, Ocular (Ptosis, Diplopia), Bulbar (Dysphagia, Dysarthria)

front: What is Diurnal Variation of Clinical Immunology?
What is Thymoma of Clinical Immunology?
back: 10-15% (Associated with AChR-Ab), CT Chest mandatory at diagnosis

front: What is Myasthenic Crisis of Clinical Immunology?
back: Respiratory failure (FVC <15-20ml/kg, MIP <30cm H2O), Bulbar weakness → ICU, IVIG/PLEX, Intubation

front: What is the definition of Clinical Immunology?
back: Parent Topic: Clinical Immunology MOC → Chapter 4 Hierarchy

front: What is the epidemiology of Clinical Immunology?
What is Timing of Clinical Immunology?
What causes Clinical Immunology?
back: Delayed adrenaline, Severe initial reaction, Inadequate initial dose, No observation

front: How is Clinical Immunology managed?
What is History of Clinical Immunology?
back: Cornerstone — Timing, Reproducibility, Co-factors (Exercise, Alcohol, NSAIDs)

front: What is SPT of Clinical Immunology?
What is sIgE of Clinical Immunology?
What is Component-Resolved Diagnostics (CRD) of Clinical Immunology?
back: Identifies specific proteins (Ara h 2 for Peanut, Ara h 8 for Cross-reactivity)

front: What is the investigation of choice for Clinical Immunology?
What is Oral Food Challenge (OFC) of Clinical Immunology?
What is Principle of Clinical Immunology?
What is Positive of Clinical Immunology?
What is Controls of Clinical Immunology?
What is Clinical Immunology indicated for?
back: Severe anaphylaxis risk, Severe uncontrolled asthma, Antihistamines (Stop 48-72h), Tricyclics, Severe Dermographism

front: What is Sensitivity/Specificity of Clinical Immunology?
What is Principle of Clinical Immunology?
What is Clinical Immunology indicated for?
back: Drug allergy (Penicillin, Venom), Aeroallergens when SPT negative but high suspicion

front: What is Positive of Clinical Immunology?
What is Sensitivity of Clinical Immunology?
back: Higher than SPT (esp. drug/venom), but ↓ specificity

front: What is Risk of Clinical Immunology?
back: Higher anaphylaxis risk than SPT — Resuscitation equipment required

front: What is Concentrations of Clinical Immunology?
What is Principle of Clinical Immunology?
What is Sensitivity/Specificity of Clinical Immunology?
What is Clinical Immunology indicated for?
back: Food/Drug Allergy (sIgE/SPT discordant), Venom, CRD Equivocal

front: What is Allergens of Clinical Immunology?
back: Baseline Series (Fragrance, Nickel, Cobalt, Potassium Dichromate, Balsam of Peru, Parabens, Formaldehyde, etc.)

front: What is Reading of Clinical Immunology?
What is Grading (ICDRG) of Clinical Immunology?
What is Irritant vs Allergic of Clinical Immunology?
back: Irritant: Fades by Day 3; Allergic: Persists/Increases

front: How is Clinical Immunology classified?
back: Type I: IgE, Mast cells, Anaphylaxis/Asthma; Type II: Antibody-mediated (AIHA, ITP); Type III: Immune complexes (SLE, Serum Sickness); Type IV: T-cell (Contact, TB, Drug); Type V: Stimulatory (Graves)

front: What is Anaphylaxis of Clinical Immunology?
back: Adrenaline IM 0.5mg (1:1000) Anterolateral Thigh — Repeat q5min; Obs 6-12h (Biphasic); Tryptase 1-2h peak

front: What is Asthma of Clinical Immunology?
back: BTS/SIGN Steps 1-6; SMART/MART (Buda/Form, Beclo/Form); Biologics: Anti-IgE, Anti-IL5/5R, Anti-IL4Rα, Anti-TSLP

front: What is Allergic Rhinitis of Clinical Immunology?
back: ARIA: Intermittent/Persistent × Mild/Mod-Severe → INCS ± H1 ± LTRA; AIT (SCIT/SLIT) 3-5y

front: What is Food Allergy of Clinical Immunology?
back: IgE (Minutes-2h) vs Non-IgE (Hours-Days); CRD (Ara h 1/2/3 = Risk, Ara h 8/9 = Cross-reactivity); OFC = Gold Standard

front: What is Drug Hypersensitivity of Clinical Immunology?
back: SJS/TEN/DRESS/AGEP — SCORTEN (TEN); Stop Drug → ICU/Supportive/IVIG/PLEX; Desensitisation (Penicillin, Aspirin, Carboplatin)

front: What is the investigation of choice for Clinical Immunology?
back: SPT (Wheal ≥3mm); sIgE (ImmunoCAP); CRD (Ara h 1/2/3 = Risk, 8/9 = Cross-reactivity); BAT (CD63/CD203c); Patch (Type IV); OFC = Gold Standard

front: What is the definition of Clinical Immunology?
back: A[Transplant Immunology] --> B[HLA SystemClass I (A,B,C) / Class II (DR, DQ, DP)]

front: What is Eplet of Clinical Immunology?
back: Polymorphic amino acid configuration on HLA surface recognised by antibodies

front: What is Eplet Mismatch Load of Clinical Immunology?
back: Number of mismatched eplets — Better predictor of DSA development than antigen mismatch

front: What is Permissible Mismatches of Clinical Immunology?
What is HLA of Clinical Immunology?
back: Class I (A,B,C) = CD8; Class II (DR,DQ,DP) = CD4; Matching: Kidney (A,B,DR), HSCT (A,B,C,DRB1,DQB1 10/10)

front: What is Allorecognition of Clinical Immunology?
back: Direct (Donor HLA on Donor APC) = Early ACR; Indirect (Donor peptides on Recipient APC) = Chronic/AMR

front: What is Rejection of Clinical Immunology?
back: Hyperacute (Pre-formed DSA, CDC-XM+); ACR (Banff t/i, Pulse Steroids); AMR (DSA+, C4d+, g+ptc, PLEX/IVIG/Rituximab); Chronic (cg, cv, IF/TA)

front: What is Immunosuppression of Clinical Immunology?
back: Induction: Basiliximab (Standard), ATG/Alemtuzumab (High Risk); Maintenance: Tac + MMF + Pred (Standard); Belatacept (CNI-Free, EBV+ only)

front: What is HSCT of Clinical Immunology?
back: MAC vs RIC; MSD > MUD > Haplo (PTCy) > Cord; aGvHD 100d (Sclerotic/Sicca/BOS)

front: What is GvHD Prophylaxis of Clinical Immunology?
back: MSD/MUD: Tac + MTX/MMF; Haplo: PTCy (Day +3, +4) + Tac + MMF (Standard)

front: How is Clinical Immunology managed?
back: aGvHD: Steroids → Ruxolitinib/ATG; cGvHD: Steroids → Ruxolitinib → ECP/Ibrutinib/Belumosudil

front: How is Clinical Immunology monitored?
back: Kidney: Cr/eGFR, Proteinuria, DSA, dd-cfDNA, BK/CMV PCR; Heart: EMB (ISHLT), DSA, dd-cfDNA; HSCT: Chimerism, Immune Reconstitution

front: What is Infection Prophylaxis of Clinical Immunology?
back: PJP (Co-trimoxazole), CMV (Valganciclovir D+/R-), HSV/VZV (Acyclovir), BKV (PCR Monitoring)

front: What is Belatacept of Clinical Immunology?
back: EBV+ Only, CNI-Free, Monthly IV, ↑ PTLD Risk if EBV-

front: What is the definition of Clinical Immunology?
back: D --> D1[Loss of Antigen PresentationMHC I Loss, B2M Mutations]

front: What is Lack of Tumour Antigens of Clinical Immunology?
back: Low TMB, No Neoantigens, MHC I Loss

front: What is Immune Exclusion of Clinical Immunology?
back: "Cold Tumour" — Excluded T cells (Stroma, VEGF, CXCL12)

front: What is Immunosuppressive TME of Clinical Immunology?
back: Tregs, MDSCs, TAMs (M2), CAFs, TGF-β, IL-10, VEGF, IDO, Adenosine

front: What is Antigen Loss of Clinical Immunology?
back: MHC I Loss (B2M Mutation), Neoantigen Loss, Antigen Presentation Defects

front: What is Checkpoint Upregulation of Clinical Immunology?
What is Signalling Bypass of Clinical Immunology?
back: JAK1/2 Mutations (IFN-γ Resistance), PTEN Loss (PI3K Activation)

front: What is Lineage Plasticity of Clinical Immunology?
What is Metabolic Adaptation of Clinical Immunology?
What is Immunoediting of Clinical Immunology?
back: Elimination → Equilibrium → Escape (MHC Loss, Checkpoint Upregulation, TME Suppression)

front: What is TSAs vs TAAs of Clinical Immunology?
What is Checkpoint Inhibitors of Clinical Immunology?
back: PD-1 (Pembro, Nivo, Cemi, Atezo, Durva, Avel); CTLA-4 (Ipi, Tremeli); LAG-3 (Relatlimab); TIGIT/TIM-3/VISTA (Investigational)

front: What is Combo Regimens of Clinical Immunology?
back: Nivo+Ipi (Mel, RCC, NSCLC); Pembro+Lenvatinib (Endometrial, RCC, HCC); Atezo+Bev (HCC)

front: What is Biomarkers of Clinical Immunology?
back: PD-L1 (TPS/CPS/IC), MSI-H/dMMR (Pan-tumour), TMB-H (≥10 mut/Mb), Neoantigen Load, IFN-γ Signature

front: What is CAR-T of Clinical Immunology?
back: CD19 (B-ALL, DLBCL, FL), BCMA (Myeloma); CRS/ICANS — Tocilizumab/Dex, Siltuximab

front: What is irAEs of Clinical Immunology?
back: Colitis, Hepatitis, Pneumonitis, Hypophysitis, Thyroiditis, Dermatitis, Myocarditis — Steroids → Infliximab (Colitis), MMF/Tac (Hepatitis), IVIG (Myocarditis/CNS)

front: What is Resistance of Clinical Immunology?
back: Primary (Cold Tumour, Low Antigen), Acquired (MHC Loss, Checkpoint Upregulation, JAK Mut, Lineage Switch)

front: What is Combinations of Clinical Immunology?
back: ICI+Chemo, ICI+Anti-VEGF, ICI+RT, ICI+Targeted, ICI+Oncolytic, Dual Checkpoint

front: What is the definition of Clinical Immunology?
back: A --> D[Cytokine TherapiesG-CSF, GM-CSF, EPO, TPO, IFN-α/β/γ]

front: What is FcRn Blockade of Clinical Immunology?
back: Saturates FcRn → ↑ Catabolism of Endogenous IgG (Including Autoantibodies)

front: What is FcγRIIb Engagement of Clinical Immunology?
What is Complement Scavenging of Clinical Immunology?
What is Idiotypic Network of Clinical Immunology?
What is Treg Expansion of Clinical Immunology?
What is Cytokine Modulation of Clinical Immunology?
back: ↓ Pro-inflammatory (TNF, IL-1, IL-6, IL-17), ↑ IL-10, IL-1Ra

front: What is FcγR Blockade of Clinical Immunology?
back: Blocks FcγR on Macrophages/Neutrophils → ↓ Phagocytosis, ADCC

front: What is Complement Inhibition of Clinical Immunology?
What is Pre-Infusion of Clinical Immunology?
back: IgG Level, Renal Function, FBC, IgA (Screen Deficiency)

front: What is During Infusion of Clinical Immunology?
back: Vital Signs q15-30min, Infusion Rate Monitoring

front: What is Post-Infusion (IVIG) of Clinical Immunology?
back: IgG Trough (Pre-Next Dose), Renal Function (If Risk)

front: What is Long-Term (SCIG) of Clinical Immunology?
back: IgG Trough q3-6m, Local Site Assessment, Patient-Reported Outcomes

front: What is Target of Clinical Immunology?
What is Clinical Immunology indicated for?
back: Atopic Dermatitis (Mod-Severe), Asthma (Type 2 High), CRSwNP, EoE (Eosinophilic Oesophagitis), Prurigo Nodularis

front: What is Dosing of Clinical Immunology?
back: 600mg Load → 300mg SC q2w (Atopic Dermatitis); 400mg Load → 200mg q2w (Asthma)

front: How is Clinical Immunology monitored?
back: Eosinophilia (Transient ↑), Parasitic Infection Risk, Herpes Zoster, Injection Site Reactions

front: What is HLA-G (Trophoblast) of Clinical Immunology?
back: Non-classical HLA — Binds ILT2/LILRB1 on NK/T Cells → Inhibition

front: What is HLA-E of Clinical Immunology?
back: Binds NKG2A/CD94 on NK Cells → Inhibition

front: What is PD-L1/PD-L2 (Trophoblast) of Clinical Immunology?
back: Binds PD-1 on T Cells → Inhibition

front: What is IDO (Tryptophan Catabolism) of Clinical Immunology?
back: Local Tryptophan Depletion → T Cell Anergy/Apoptosis

front: What is Treg Expansion of Clinical Immunology?
back: FoxP3+ Tregs ↑ in Decidua → IL-10, TGF-β, IL-35

front: What is HLA-C (Paternal) of Clinical Immunology?
back: KIR-HLA Interactions on uNK Cells → Licensing/Inhibition Balance

front: What is the pathogenesis of Clinical Immunology?
back: RhD-Negative Mother → Sensitisation (Feto-Maternal Haemorrhage) → Anti-D IgG → Crosses Placenta → Haemolysis of RhD+ve Fetus

front: What is Prevention of Clinical Immunology?
back: Anti-D IgG Prophylaxis: 28w + 34w (Routine), Post-Delivery (72h), Sensitising Events (Bleeding, Amnio, Trauma)

front: How is Clinical Immunology monitored?
back: Maternal Anti-D Titre (q4w 28-32w, q2w 28-34w), MCA Doppler (MCA-PSV >1.5 MoM = Anaemia)

front: How is Clinical Immunology managed?
back: Intrauterine Transfusion (IUT), Phototherapy, Exchange Transfusion, IVIG (Adjunct)

front: What is the pathogenesis of Clinical Immunology?
back: Uncontrolled T/NK activation → Cytokine storm (IFN-γ, IL-6, IL-18, IL-10, CXCL9/10) → Macrophage activation → Phagocytosis of blood cells (Haemophagocytosis)

front: What is Triggers of Clinical Immunology?
back: Primary (Familial): PRF1, UNC13D, STX11, STXBP2, RAB27A perforin defects; Secondary: Infection (EBV, CMV), Malignancy (Lymphoma), Autoimmune (SLE, sJIA = MAS), Drugs

front: What is HLH-2004 Criteria of Clinical Immunology?
back: ≥5 of 8: Fever, Splenomegaly, Cytopenias (≥2 lineages), Hypertriglyceridaemia / Hypofibrinogenaemia, Haemophagocytosis (BM/Spleen/LN), Low/absent NK activity, Ferritin ≥500 µg/L (often >>10,000), sCD25 (sIL-2Rα) ≥2400 U/mL

front: What is HScore of Clinical Immunology?
back: Risk score for secondary HLH (0-266): Temperature, Organomegaly, Cytopenias, Ferritin, Triglycerides, Fibrinogen, AST, Haemophagocytosis, Immunosuppression; ≥169 = 93% prob. HLH; ≥200 = 99% prob.

front: How is Clinical Immunology managed?
back: HLH-94 Protocol: Dexamethasone + Etoposide + Intrathecal Methotrexate; Anakinra (IL-1Ra) for MAS/sJIA; Ruxolitinib (JAK1/2i) for refractory; Eculizumab (C5i) for CAPS-associated; HSCT for familial/refractory

front: What is Adipose Tissue Expansion of Clinical Immunology?
back: Hypertrophy → Hypoxia → Necrosis → Crown-Like Structures (Macrophage Crowns)

front: What is Macrophage Polarisation of Clinical Immunology?
back: M1 (Pro-inflammatory) Dominance in VAT (Visceral Adipose Tissue) → TNF-α, IL-6, IL-1β, MCP-1

front: What is Adipokine Dysregulation of Clinical Immunology?
What is Insulin Resistance of Clinical Immunology?
back: JNK/IKKβ/NF-κB Activation → Serine Phosphorylation of IRS-1 → Impaired Insulin Signalling

front: What is Gut-Liver Axis of Clinical Immunology?
back: Dysbiosis → LPS/TLR4 → Kupffer Cell Activation → Hepatic Inflammation (NAFLD/NASH)

front: What is SCFAs (Butyrate, Propionate, Acetate) of Clinical Immunology?
back: HDAC Inhibition → Treg Expansion, Barrier Integrity, Anti-inflammatory

front: What is Bile Acid Metabolism of Clinical Immunology?
back: FXR/FXR Activation → Metabolic Regulation, Immune Modulation

front: What is Tryptophan Metabolism of Clinical Immunology?
back: AhR Ligands (Indoles) → IL-22, IL-17, Barrier Function

front: What is Bile Acid Transformation of Clinical Immunology?
What is Molecular Mimicry of Clinical Immunology?
What is the definition of Clinical Immunology?
back: Epigenetic Reprogramming of Innate Immune Cells (Monocytes, NK Cells) → Enhanced Response to Secondary Stimulus

front: What is Inducers of Clinical Immunology?
back: BCG, β-Glucan, Measles Vaccine, BCG Scarification

front: What is Epigenetic Changes of Clinical Immunology?
back: H3K4me3, H3K27ac at Promoters of Inflammatory Genes (IL-6, TNF-α, IL-1β)

front: What is Clinical Relevance of Clinical Immunology?
back: BCG → ↓ Neonatal Mortality, BCG → ↓ Respiratory Infections, BCG → ↓ COVID-19 Severity (Debated)

front: What is Recurrent C. difficile Infection (rCDI) of Clinical Immunology?
What is IBD (UC) of Clinical Immunology?
What is Obesity/Metabolic Syndrome of Clinical Immunology?
What is Autism/Neuropsychiatric of Clinical Immunology?
What is Target of Clinical Immunology?
What is Platform of Clinical Immunology?
What is Schedule of Clinical Immunology?
What is Efficacy of Clinical Immunology?
back: ~30-40% against Clinical Malaria (4 years); ~50% in first year; ~30% against Severe Malaria

front: What is WHO Recommendation of Clinical Immunology?
back: Broad Implementation in Sub-Saharan Africa (2021) — Pilot Rollout: Ghana, Kenya, Malawi

front: What is Impact of Clinical Immunology?
back: Reduces Severe Malaria Hospitalisations; Cost-Effective alongside ITNs, SMC, IRS

front: What is Limitations of Clinical Immunology?
back: Waning Immunity (Requires Booster); Not 100% Protective; Cold Chain Required

front: What is Immunosenescence of Clinical Immunology?
back: Thymic Involution → ↓ Naive T Cells, ↑ Memory/Senescent T Cells, Inflammageing (↑ IL-6, TNF, CRP), CMV Accelerates; Vaccine Response ↓ → High-Dose/Adjuvanted Vaccines

front: What is Pregnancy Immunology of Clinical Immunology?
back: Maternal-Fetal Tolerance: HLA-G, HLA-E, PD-L1, IDO, Tregs; Autoimmune in Pregnancy: SLE (Flare Risks, HCQ Safe), APS (LMWH+Aspirin), RA (Improves, Certolizumab Safe), IBD (Continue Biologics), MS (Relapse Postpartum)

front: What is Drug Safety in Pregnancy of Clinical Immunology?
back: HCQ Safe, AZA Safe, MTX/LEF/MMF/CYC Contraindicated, TNFi: Certolizumab Safe (No Fc), Others Stop 20-24w, JAKi Contraindicated, Rituximab Avoid 3rd Trimester

front: What is RHD of Clinical Immunology?
back: Anti-D Prophylaxis (28/34w, Post-Delivery, Sensitising Events), MCA Doppler, IUT/Phototherapy/Exchange

front: What is Vaccines in Pregnancy of Clinical Immunology?
back: Tdap (16-32w), Flu (Any Trimester), COVID, RSV (28-36w); Live Vaccines Contraindicated

front: What is Critical Illness of Clinical Immunology?
back: Hyperinflammatory → Immunoparalysis; mHLA-DR <30% = Immunoparalysis; Biomarkers: mHLA-DR, Lymphopenia, PD-1, IL-10; Therapies: IFN-γ, GM-CSF, IL-7 (Experimental)

front: What is Obesity of Clinical Immunology?
back: Metaflammation: VAT → M1 Macrophages → TNF/IL-6/IL-1β → Insulin Resistance; Vaccine Response ↓; ↑ Infection/Cancer/Autoimmunity Risk

front: What is Microbiome of Clinical Immunology?
back: SCFAs → Treg ↑, Barrier ↑; Dysbiosis: IBD, RA, T1DM, MS, Obesity; Trained Immunity (BCG, β-Glucan → Epigenetic Reprogramming); FMT (rCDI >90%)

front: What is Vaccines of Clinical Immunology?
back: Platforms: Live Attenuated, Inactivated, Subunit, Conjugate, Viral Vector, mRNA, Protein Subunit; Cold Chain (mRNA: -70°C/-20°C); Contraindications: Live in Immunocompromised/Pregnancy

front: What is UK Schedule of Clinical Immunology?
back: 8/12/16w: 6-in-1, Rotavirus, MenB, PCV; 1yr: MMR, Hib/MenC, PCV, MenB; 3y4m: MMR, 4-in-1; 12-13y: HPV; 14y: MenACWY, Td/IPV; ≥65: Flu (HD/aQIV), PPV/PCV20, Shingrix, RSV

front: What is Vaccine Hesitancy of Clinical Immunology?
back: 3 Cs: Confidence (Trust), Complacency (Risk Perception), Convenience (Access); Equity: Targeted Outreach, Multilingual, Cultural Safety

back: TMPRSS6 mutation → ↑hepcidin → oral Fe ineffective; IV Fe partial

back: Acquired: MDS (SF3B1), alcohol, B6, lead; Inherited: ALAS2 (X-linked)

back: Wheat bran, cellulose, hemicellulose, lignin (whole grains, veg skins, nuts)

front: What is Soluble viscous (gel) fibres?
back: β-glucan, psyllium, pectin, guar gum, glucomannan

front: What is Prebiotic =?
back: Substrate selectively utilised by host microorganisms conferring health benefit (ISAPP 2017)

front: What is Established prebiotics (4)?
What is IBS fibre: YES / NO?
back: YES: Psyllium, oat β-glucan, linseed; NO: Wheat bran, inulin, FOS, GOS, resistant starch

front: What is Diverticular prevention?
back: >25 g/day total fibre; nuts/seeds NOT contraindicated (myth busted)

front: What is SCFA butyrate?
back: Colonocyte fuel (70% energy), HDAC inhibitor (anti-cancer, anti-inflammatory), tight junctions, apoptosis

front: What is Psyllium mechanism LDL-C ↓?

front: What is Psyllium drug separation?

back: RS1 (inaccessible), RS2 (raw granules), RS3 (retrograded cooled), RS4 (chemically modified)

back: Viscous soluble 10–15 g/day (psyllium, β-glucan) → HbA1c ↓0.5%, postprandial glucose ↓20–30%

front: What is Lactulose in hepatic encephalopathy?
back: ↓colonic pH → NH₄⁺ trapped → ↓NH₃ absorption; 2–3 soft stools/day; also prebiotic

front: Define total energy expenditure (TEE) and its components.?
back: TEE = BMR (60–75 %) + TEF (~10 %) + PAEE (15–30 %) + (growth / pregnancy / lactation).

front: What is the clinical "rule-of-thumb" BMR in adults?
Strongest single determinant of BMR?
Effect of fever on BMR?
back: ↑ by ~ 10–13 % per 1 °C rise in core temperature (Q10 effect).

front: Mifflin–St Jeor for men?
back: BMR = 10·W + 6.25·H − 5·A + 5 (W kg, H cm, A yrs).

TEF for protein / CHO / fat?
PAL for a sedentary office worker?

WHO BMI cut-offs for overweight / obese (general population)?
South Asian BMI cut-offs (WHO 2004)?

RQ during lipogenesis (overfeeding)?
Principle of doubly-labelled water?
back: ²H₂¹⁸O; difference in ²H and ¹⁸O elimination = CO₂ production → TEE (over 7–14 d, free-living).

front: Indications for indirect calorimetry?
back: ICU ventilated patients, severe malnutrition, refeeding risk, obesity, research.

Energy cost of 1 kg of adipose tissue?
Energy density of oil vs apple?

back: Plant sterols 2g + viscous fibre 10g + soy protein 25g + nuts 45g = ↓LDL 30%

front: What is Trans fat harm?
back: Industrial trans: ↑LDL + ↓HDL = double CVD risk; WHO <1% energy

front: What is the conversion factor of dietary protein to nitrogen for a mixed diet?
back: 1 g N ≡ 6.25 g protein (used in Kjeldahl analysis and N balance). Milk 6.38; wheat 5.83; rice 5.95; soy 5.71; egg 6.25.

front: How many essential amino acids are there in adults? List them.?
back: 9. Histidine, Isoleucine, Leucine, Lysine, Methionine, Phenylalanine, Threonine, Tryptophan, Valine.

front: List the 6 conditionally essential amino acids.?
back: Arginine, Cysteine, Glutamine, Glycine, Proline, Tyrosine.

front: What are the BCAAs and where are they primarily metabolised?
back: Leucine, Isoleucine, Valine — metabolised mainly in skeletal muscle by BCAT.

front: What is the role of leucine in muscle?
back: Activates mTORC1 → ↑ muscle protein synthesis, ↓ proteolysis.

front: What is biological value (BV) and the reference standard?
back: % of absorbed protein retained. Reference = 100. Egg 100, Whey 104, Casein 91, Soy 91, Wheat 54.

front: Difference between PDCAAS and DIAAS?
back: PDCAAS uses faecal digestibility, capped at 1.0 (FDA). DIAAS uses ileal digestibility, no cap, can exceed 1.0 (FAO 2013).

front: Limiting amino acid in wheat? In legumes?
What does the F-75 formula provide vs F-100 in SAM management?
back: F-75 = 75 kcal/100 mL, 0.9 g protein/100 mL (stabilisation). F-100 = 100 kcal/100 mL, 2.9 g protein/100 mL (rehabilitation).

front: Why does kwashiorkor cause a fatty liver?
back: ApoB-100 deficiency (↓ apoprotein synthesis) → failure of VLDL assembly/secretion → triglycerides accumulate.

front: Name 4 clinical states producing negative nitrogen balance.?
back: Trauma, burns, sepsis, prolonged fasting/hyperthyroidism/advanced cancer.

back: Sodium benzoate (→ hippurate) and sodium phenylbutyrate/phenylacetate (→ phenylacetyl-glutamine).

front: Why are BCAA-enriched formulae used in hepatic encephalopathy?
back: They reduce aromatic AA (Phe, Tyr, Trp) competition at the blood–brain barrier; decrease brain false neurotransmitters; may improve mental status and reduce recurrence.

front: What is the energy yield of protein per gram?
back: 4 kcal/g (17 kJ/g) gross, but ~3 kcal/g net (≈ 1 kcal lost in urea synthesis).

front: What is the limiting AA in egg compared with human requirement?
back: None — egg is the classical reference (no limiting AA); slightly low in tryptophan by modern standards but otherwise complete.

back: Phosphate + calcitriol; Burosumab (anti-FGF23 mAb); NO high-dose vit D

back: Type I: CYP27B1 defect → low 1,25-D → calcitriol works; Type II: VDR defect → high 1,25-D + alopecia

front: What is CKD vit D?

back: 50k IU weekly ×8w → maintenance 1500–2000 IU/day D3; target 25(OH)D >30 ng/mL

front: What is Neuro triad?

front: What is Abetalipoproteinaemia?

front: What is AVED (TTPA)?
back: Normal lipids/lipoproteins; plasma α-toc <5% normal; Tx: RRR-α-toc 800–1200 IU/day lifelong

front: What is Preterm haemolytic anaemia?
back: Low vit E stores + high O₂ + iron → lipid peroxidation of RBC membranes; Tx: 10–20 IU/kg/day

front: What is SELECT trial?
back: 35,533 men; vit E 400 IU/day + Se 200 µg → ↑prostate cancer HR 1.17; stopped early

back: UL 1000 mg (1500 IU natural); RRR-α-toc 2x bioactivity of all-rac

front: What is γ-tocopherol?
back: Major dietary form (soybean/corn oil); traps RNS (NO₂•, ONOO⁻); anti-inflammatory

front: What is PT vs aPTT in vit K deficiency?
back: PT/INR ↑ FIRST (extrinsic, VII); aPTT ↑ LATE (intrinsic, IX, X, II)
