front: What is ucOC?
back: Uncarboxylated osteocalcin; ucOC/total OC ratio = vit K status

front: What is MK-7 advantages?
back: Half-life 72h (vs K1 1–2h); extrahepatic; better osteocalcin/MGP carboxylation

front: What is Superwarfarin?
back: Brodifacoum; irreversible VKORC1 inhibition; Tx: K1 50–100 mg/day ×weeks–months

front: What is Coumarin resistance?
back: VKORC1 mutation (-1639G>A); warfarin resistance; high-dose warfarin needed

front: What is Bone vit K status?
back: ucOC/total OC ratio; DP-ucMGP for vascular status

What is PA sensitive antibody?

back: PAID = PA + T1DM, Addison, Immune (Hashimoto, vitiligo)

front: HIV CD4 threshold for live vaccines?

front: WHO 3Cs of vaccine hesitancy?

front: Mandate ethics (4 principles)?

front: Yellow Card country?

front: Saudi Grand Mufti 2021 fatwa?
back: mRNA vaccines halal, permissible, do not break fast

front: Mature minor doctrine?

front: Vaccine immunity pathway?

front: Th2 signature cytokines & function?

R₀ = 4 → HIT?

topic_title: Vaccine Types Live Inactivated Subunit Mrna Vector

back: MMR, Varicella, YF 17D, BCG, OPV, Rotavirus, LAIV, oral typhoid Ty21a, oral cholera

topic_title: Vaccine Types Live Inactivated Subunit Mrna Vector

back: Whole pathogen killed (heat/formalin/BPL) → no replication; safe, weaker response, needs adjuvant + multiple doses

topic_title: Vaccine Types Live Inactivated Subunit Mrna Vector

back: IPV, Hep A, Rabies, Injectable flu, WC-pertussis, Cholera WC-rBS, Typhoid Vi, JE Vero, TBE, Sinovac

topic_title: Vaccine Types Live Inactivated Subunit Mrna Vector

topic_title: Vaccine Types Live Inactivated Subunit Mrna Vector

back: Polysaccharide = T-independent, IgM dominant, no memory, no class switch, <2y fails. Conjugate = T-dependent, IgG, memory, all ages

topic_title: Vaccine Types Live Inactivated Subunit Mrna Vector

back: Nucleoside-modified mRNA in LNP → host ribosomes translate antigen → MHC I + II presentation; transient, no DNA integration

topic_title: Vaccine Types Live Inactivated Subunit Mrna Vector

topic_title: Vaccine Types Live Inactivated Subunit Mrna Vector

back: Disseminated/meningeal TB in children; variable efficacy 0–80% pulmonary TB; not adults

topic_title: Travel Medicine Pre Travel Assessment Prophylaxis
front: VFR traveller — risk profile?
back: Highest risk of malaria, typhoid, Hep A/B, TB, STI; lowest pre-travel consultation rate.

topic_title: Travel Medicine Pre Travel Assessment Prophylaxis
front: Hajj-specific health advice (3)?
back: Hand hygiene, mask (MERS), avoid camels, heat precautions, OCP stop 4 wks pre if possible, ensure drug supply.

topic_title: Travel Medicine Pre Travel Assessment Prophylaxis
front: Pregnancy + travel to malaria area 2nd trimester — best chemoprophylaxis for CQ-R area?
back: Mefloquine (limited safety data; AP contraindicated; doxy contraindicated; CQ for sensitive areas).

topic_title: Travel Medicine Pre Travel Assessment Prophylaxis
front: Asplenic traveller — what extra vaccines?
back: MenACWY + MenB, pneumococcal (PCV + PPSV23), Hib (paed), annual influenza.

topic_title: Travel Medicine Pre Travel Assessment Prophylaxis
front: Returning fever first test?
back: Thick and thin blood films for malaria × 3 (q12–24 h) + RDT — within 24 h.

topic_title: Travel Medicine Pre Travel Assessment Prophylaxis
front: Fever + eschar returning from Asia?
back: Scrub typhus (or other rickettsial); empirical doxycycline 100 mg BD × 7 d.

topic_title: Travel Medicine Pre Travel Assessment Prophylaxis
front: Hajj returnee with fever + meningism + petechial rash?
back: Meningococcal meningitis → ceftriaxone 2 g IV stat + close-contact prophylaxis + notify.

topic_title: Travel Medicine Pre Travel Assessment Prophylaxis
front: Notifiable travel-acquired infections (5)?
back: Malaria, typhoid, hepatitis A/B/C, TB, meningococcal, cholera, VHF, rabies, plague, anthrax, COVID-19, Zika, chikungunya, monkeypox, leishmaniasis, schistosomiasis.

topic_title: Travel Medicine Pre Travel Assessment Prophylaxis
front: Food/water rules for travellers?
back: "Boil it, peel it, cook it, or forget it" — sealed/bottled/boiled/filtered water; hot food hot, cold food cold; no ice from unknown water; avoid shellfish and unpasteurised dairy.

topic_title: Travel Medicine Pre Travel Assessment Prophylaxis
front: Chloroquine — when still used?
back: Only for CQ-sensitive areas: Central America west of Panama, Caribbean, ME, Egypt, Sri Lanka, parts of China, Korea.

topic_title: Travel Medicine Pre Travel Assessment Prophylaxis
front: BCG for travel?
back: Not routinely; only select infants/children in some national programmes.

topic_title: Travel Medicine Pre Travel Assessment Prophylaxis
front: 4–6-week timing allows what?
back: Vaccine series completion, YF certificate (10 d), malaria drug trial and supply, G6PD test, behavioural advice, accelerated schedules if needed.

topic_title: Travel Medicine Pre Travel Assessment Prophylaxis
front: Special pops — which live vaccines in pregnancy?
back: YF, MMR, varicella, LAIV, oral typhoid Ty21a, oral cholera, JE-VC are all live attenuated; use with caution or avoid in pregnancy.

topic_title: Travel Medicine Pre Travel Assessment Prophylaxis
front: Pre-exposure rabies — when indicated and schedule?
back: Rural/animal-contact/trekking; 0/7/21–28 d IM (or 0/7/21 ID); simplifies PEP to 2 doses days 0/3, no RIG.

topic_title: Travel Medicine Pre Travel Assessment Prophylaxis
front: Japanese encephalitis vaccine — when indicated?
back: Rural Asia, ≥ 1-month stay, rice farming, pig contact.

topic_title: Travel Medicine Pre Travel Assessment Prophylaxis
front: Typhoid Vi (injectable) vs Ty21a (oral) — differences?
back: Vi = single IM dose ≥ 2 y, boost 2–3 y; Ty21a = 4 oral doses ≥ 6 y, boost 5 y. Ty21a live attenuated (CI immunocompromised, antibiotics). Neither covers paratyphoid.

topic_title: Travel Medicine Pre Travel Assessment Prophylaxis
front: Cholera vaccine — when recommended?
back: Humanitarian workers, refugees, disaster relief — Dukoral 2 doses 1 wk apart.

topic_title: Travel Medicine Pre Travel Assessment Prophylaxis
front: Travellers with HIV CD4 250 — can they have YF vaccine?
back: Yes with caution (precaution, not absolute CI) if asymptomatic and CD4 200–500; contraindicated if CD4 < 200. ---

front: What is the definition of CNS Tumours?
back: WHO CNS5 Grade 4 diffuse astrocytic glioma; IDH-wildtype (90%) or IDH-mutant (10%)

front: What is the epidemiology of CNS Tumours?
back: 3-4/100,000/year; Most common primary malignant brain tumour (50% of gliomas)

front: What is Peak Age of CNS Tumours?
What is Sex Ratio of CNS Tumours?
What causes CNS Tumours?
back: Ionising Radiation (prior RT), Genetic Syndromes (Li-Fraumeni - TP53, Neurofibromatosis Type 1, Turcot/Lynch), No proven environmental link

front: What is Headache of CNS Tumours?
What is Seizures of CNS Tumours?

front: What is Recurrent of CNS Tumours?
back: EF-11 Trial: Non-Inferior to Chemo, Better QoL

front: What is Technique of CNS Tumours?

front: What is TTFields (Optune) of CNS Tumours?

back: <6mo Post-RT, Stable Clinically, Regresses on Follow-Up — Do Not Switch Therapy Prematurely

back: Levetiracetam (No CYP Interaction); Avoid Enzyme Inducers

front: What is Transplantation of Survivorship & Late Effects?
What is the prognosis of Survivorship & Late Effects?
back: >200 Live Births Worldwide (ASRM: No Longer Experimental); Endocrine Function Restoration (90%+), Fertility Restoration (~30-40% Pregnancy Rate)

front: What is Risks of Survivorship & Late Effects?
back: Reintroduction of Malignant Cells (Screen Tissue: Histology, Molecular Markers); Surgical Complications

front: What is Procedure of Survivorship & Late Effects?
back: Laparoscopic → Move Ovaries Laterally/Superiorly (Outside Radiation Field) → Fix to Abdominal Wall/Peritoneum → Clip/Suture Markers for Future Localisation

front: What is Timing of Survivorship & Late Effects?
back: Before RT (Ideally >4-6 Weeks Before for Vascular Recovery)

front: What is the definition of Upper GI Cancers?
back: Malignant epithelial tumour of stomach; Adenocarcinoma >90%; classified by Lauren (Intestinal vs Diffuse) and WHO (Tubular, Papillary, Mucinous, Signet-ring, Poorly cohesive)

front: What is the epidemiology of Upper GI Cancers?
back: UK: ~6,500/year; Global: 5th most common cancer; M:F = 2:1; Declining in West, high in East Asia (Korea, Japan, China)

front: What is Peak Age of Upper GI Cancers?
What is Sex Ratio of Upper GI Cancers?
What causes Upper GI Cancers?
back: H. pylori (Class I carcinogen, ↑6x), Smoking, Salt/smoked foods, Nitrosamines, Pernicious anaemia (atrophic gastritis), Blood group A, Obesity (cardia), Familial: HDGC (CDH1), Lynch, FAP, GAPPS

front: How is Upper GI Cancers classified?
back: Intestinal (exophytic, H. pylori, older, better prognosis, HER2+) vs Diffuse (infiltrative, signet-ring, CDH1, younger, peritoneal spread, CLDN18.2+)

front: What is Linitis Plastica of Upper GI Cancers?
back: Diffuse type; rigid stomach; T4a; poor prognosis; often inoperable at presentation

front: What is H. pylori of Upper GI Cancers?
back: Class I carcinogen; Eradication ↓ metachronous cancer risk; Test & treat

front: What is FLOT of Upper GI Cancers?
back: Standard perioperative for resectable (T2+ or N+): Docetaxel/Oxaliplatin/5-FU/LV q2w ×4 pre + 4 post

front: What is HER2+ of Upper GI Cancers?
What is CLDN18.2+ of Upper GI Cancers?
What is MSI-H/dMMR of Upper GI Cancers?
What is EBV+ of Upper GI Cancers?
back: 9%; PD-L1↑, PIK3CA mut; Good ICI response

front: What is D2 Lymphadenectomy of Upper GI Cancers?
back: ≥15 nodes examined; Standard for curative; Spleen/pancreas preservation

front: What is Peritoneal Mets of Upper GI Cancers?
back: M1; Diffuse type; HIPEC/PIPAC experimental; Poor prognosis

front: What is Hereditary of Upper GI Cancers?
back: HDGC (CDH1): Prophylactic total gastrectomy age 20-30; Lynch (MMR), FAP (APC), GAPPS (APC)

front: What is the definition of Urological Cancers?
back: Bladder cancer = Urothelial carcinoma (90%); NMIBC (Ta/Tis/T1) = 75% vs MIBC (T2-4) = 25%.

front: What is Haematuria of Urological Cancers?
What is NMIBC Risk of Urological Cancers?
back: EAU/EORTC: Low / Intermediate / High (based on grade, stage, size, number, recurrence, CIS)

front: What is BCG Regimen of Urological Cancers?
back: Induction 6 weekly → Maintenance SWOG 8507: 3 weekly at 3,6,12,18,24,30,36mo (Total 27)

front: What is BCG-Unresponsive of Urological Cancers?
back: CIS recurrence at 6mo / Ta/T1 HG at 6mo / T1 HG at 3mo after adequate BCG → Cystectomy or ICI/Enfortumab

front: What is Re-TURBT of Urological Cancers?
back: Mandatory for T1 (10-30% upstaging to T2) and incomplete resection

front: What is MIBC of Urological Cancers?
back: Neoadjuvant Cisplatin (GC preferred) → Radical Cystectomy + Extended PLND → Adjuvant Nivo if ypT1-4/ypN+

front: What is Cisplatin Eligibility of Urological Cancers?
back: Galsky: GFR<60, ECOG≥2, Neuropathy≥2, Hearing≥2, HF Class III/IV

front: What is Metastatic 1L of Urological Cancers?
What is 2L Metastatic of Urological Cancers?
back: Enfortumab Vedotin (anti-Nectin-4 ADC), Erdafitinib (FGFR3 mut), Sacituzumab Govitecan (TROP-2 ADC)

front: What is Upper Tract UC (UTUC) of Urological Cancers?
back: Same biology; Nephroureterectomy with bladder cuff; Adjuvant chemo if high risk; Surveillance for bladder recurrence

front: What is the definition of Cancer Screening & Prevention?
back: Chemoprevention: Aspirin (CRC, Lynch CAPP2 63% Reduction), Tamoxifen/Raloxifene (Breast, IBIS-I/STAR), Aromatase Inhibitors (Anastrozole/Exemestane, IBIS-II/MAP.3); Lifestyle: Tobacco, Alcohol, Obesity, Diet, Exercise, UV; Aspirin: General Population Modest Benefit, Lynch 600mg×2yr 63% Reduction (CAPP2).

What is Vitamin D of Cancer Screening & Prevention?
What is NSAIDs (Non-Aspirin) of Cancer Screening & Prevention?
What is Statins of Cancer Screening & Prevention?

back: Tamoxifen 20mg ×5yr (IBIS-I, NSABP P-1); Raloxifene 60mg ×5yr (STAR: Non-inferior, Less Endometrial/Thrombotic); Anastrozole 1mg ×5yr (IBIS-II: 53% RR ↓); Exemestane 25mg ×5yr (MAP.3: 65% RR ↓)

front: What is the definition of Cancer of the Breast?
back: Breast cancer = commonest cancer in women worldwide.

front: How is Cancer of the Breast classified?

front: What is Luminal B HER2- of Cancer of the Breast?

front: What is pCR of Cancer of the Breast?

front: How is Cancer of the Breast managed?

front: What is TARE (Y-90) of Hepatobiliary & Pancreatic?

front: What are the complications of Hepatobiliary & Pancreatic?
back: Variceal bleed (prophylactic banding), HE, HRS, Tumour rupture (emergency TACE)

back: Cancer = dysregulated cell growth due to accumulated genetic/epigenetic alterations → autonomous proliferation, invasion, metastasis

back: MYC Amplification (Radiation-Associated, >90%), KDR/VEGFR2 Mut, PTPRB, PLCG1

back: CD31+, ERG+, FLI1+, CD34+, Factor VIII+

front: What is Behaviour of Sarcomas?
back: Aggressive, Multifocal, Early Haematogenous Spread (Liver, Lung)

front: How is Sarcomas managed?
What is Metastatic of Sarcomas?
back: Paclitaxel (Weekly) + Bevacizumab — Best Response, Doxorubicin-based, Pembrolizumab (Immunotherapy), Sorafenib/Pazopanib (VEGFR TKI)

topic_title: Non Melanoma Skin Cancer Bcc Scc Merkel Cell
front: What is the definition of Skin Cancer?
back: NMSC = Most Common Cancers (BCC > SCC); BCC 75-80%, SCC 20-25%, Merkel Cell <1%; UVR Primary Risk; BCC: PTCH1/SMO (Hedgehog), Local Destruction, Surgery (Mohs), Vismodegib/Sonidegib (Advanced); SCC: UVR, Immunosuppression, TP53, Surgery (Mohs), RT, Cemiplimab (Advanced); Merkel Cell: MCPyV, Neuroendocrine, Aggressive, Surgery + RT, ICI (Avelumab/Pembro); High-Risk Features: Size, Depth, PNI, Poor
